articles.author: David Koop, Sara Aboubakr
articles.description: Regional powers plan to meet Sunday in Pakistan to discuss how to end the fighting in the Middle East as about 2,500 U.S. Marines arrived in the region and Iranian-backed Houthi rebels entered the monthlong war.
articles.title: Live updates: Iran warns the US against a ground invasion as regional powers meet in Pakistan

articles.author: Nicole Winfield
articles.description: Pope Leo XIV is celebrating Palm Sunday before tens of thousands of people in St. Peter’s Square. The celebration kicks off Holy Week. It began with a procession of cardinals, bishops, priests and laypeople walking into the square carrying olive branches and palm fronds, some intricately braided. Palm Sunday marks Jesus’ triumphant entrance into Jerusalem in the time leading up to his crucifixion, which Christians observe on Good Friday, and resurrection on Easter Sunday. For many people, Leo's first Holy Week recalls the final, suffering days of Pope Francis’ life. He died April 21 last year, on what was Easter Monday.
articles.title: Pope Leo XIV opens Holy Week with Palm Sunday that recalls final days of Pope Francis' life

articles.author: Melanie Lidman, Nicole Winfield
articles.description: The Latin Patriarchate says Israeli police have blocked top Catholic leaders from entering Jerusalem’s Church of the Holy Sepulchre for Palm Sunday Mass. Israel's police says safety and emergency access concerns drive the decision and that the church doesn't have sufficient shelter spaces in case of an Iranian missile. The Patriarchate canceled the traditional processional last week because of safety concerns and has held Masses limited to fewer than 50 worshippers in compliance with the Israeli military’s guidelines for civilians. Italy condemned the decision and summoned the Israeli ambassador for questioning.
articles.title: Israeli police prevent Palm Sunday Mass at Jerusalem church over safety concerns

articles.author: Phil Holm, Kevin S. Vineys, WILL JARRETT
articles.description: How the Strait of Hormuz, a tiny stretch of water, quietly powers a huge part of the global economy
articles.title: The world’s most important 21 miles

articles.author: Mark Vancleave, Steve Karnowski, Todd Richmond, John Hanna
articles.description: Tens of thousands of people have joined “No Kings” protests the war in Iran and President Donald Trump’s actions. Organizers held hundreds of rallies Saturday across the U.S. and in Europe, with Minnesota taking center stage and organizers expecting millions of people to participate. Thousands of demonstrators stood packed shoulder-to-shoulder on the Minnesota Capitol lawn and surrounding streets in St. Paul. Some upside down U.S. flags, historically a sign of distress. The event’s headliner was Bruce Springsteen, who sang “Streets of Minneapolis” to honor Minnesota residents who this winter protested Trump's aggressive immigration enforcement efforts. Washington, New York, London, Paris and Rome had protests, but there also were rallies in smaller cities and towns.
articles.title: Minnesota leads wave of anti-Trump protests across US, in Europe

articles.author: The Associated Press
articles.description: ST. PAUL, Minn. (AP) — The third round of “No Kings” protests against President Donald Trump's administration is happening Saturday across the U.S., with Minnesota hosting the flagship rally in St.
articles.title: Photos of "No Kings" rallies against Trump

articles.author: Mark Sherman
articles.description: Some names will be familiar to the Supreme Court in the latest case involving a Black death row inmate from Mississippi. One is Doug Evans, a now-retired prosecutor with a history of dismissing Black jurors for discriminatory reasons. He knocked all but one Black person off the jury in the case set for arguments Tuesday. State Judge Joseph Loper allowed those juror challenges. And the state Supreme Court upheld the conviction. Just seven years ago, in a case involving the same prosecutor, trial judge and state high court, the Supreme Court overturned a different conviction and death sentence because of what Justice Brett Kavanaugh described as a “relentless, determined effort to rid the jury of Black individuals.”
articles.title: The Supreme Court takes up the Mississippi case of a Black death row inmate

articles.author: Stephen Groves, Mary Clare Jalonick, Joey Cappelletti
articles.description: There seemed to be a deal. Senators appeared to have finally reached an agreement to fund most of the Department of Homeland Security and effectively end a partial government shutdown that's dragged on for more than a month. But then it quickly collapsed after House Speaker Mike Johnson dismissed the deal as a “joke.” His criticism amounted to a denunciation of a deal that his counterpart, Senate Majority Leader John Thune, had orchestrated. The misalignment between the two Republican leaders is a sign of the difficulty ahead for Republicans as they try to maneuver President Donald Trump’s priorities through Congress.
articles.title: How the Homeland Security deal unraveled and split Republican leaders

articles.author: JoNel Aleccia, Amy Taxin
articles.description: California is the first state to require food makers to add folic acid, a crucial vitamin, to corn masa flour used to make tortillas and other foods. It's a move aimed at reducing Hispanic infants' disproportionately high rates of rare and severe birth defects. A similar law takes effect in Alabama in June, with legislation pending or being considered in Florida, Georgia, Oklahoma and Oregon. Opponents see the mandate as government overreach or claim folic acid is toxic, without evidence. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy criticized California's law. But medical and nutrition experts praised it as a public health triumph.
articles.title: Corn tortillas in California must now contain folic acid

articles.author: Marcia Dunn
articles.description: NASA's Apollo moonshots are a tough act to follow, even after all this time. As four astronauts get set to blast off on humanity's first trip to the moon in more than half a century, comparisons between Apollo and NASA's new Artemis program are inevitable. Artemis reflects more of society, with a woman, person of color and Canadian rocketing away from Florida's Kennedy Space Center. But they won't orbit the moon like the world's first lunar visitors did on Apollo 8 back in 1968. Instead, they'll play it safe and zip around the moon in an out-and-back slingshot like Apollo 13.
articles.title: Apollo vs. Artemis: What to know about NASA's moon missions
